slides.title: Module 2 — Personal Hygiene & GMPs
slides.subtitle: SQF Food Safety Training
slides.notes: Welcome to Module 2. In Module 1 we covered why we're getting SQF certified and the part everyone plays. Now we get practical. This module is about personal hygiene and good manufacturing practices — the everyday habits that keep our food safe. These are the basics an auditor expects to see on the floor, every shift.

slides.title: Learning Objectives
slides.lead: By the end of this module you will be able to:

items: Explain what GMPs are and why they protect our food
Wash and sanitize your hands the right way, at the right times
Follow our attire, jewelry, nail, and illness rules
Move between zones without spreading contamination

slides.notes: Here's what you'll walk away with. Four things: what good manufacturing practices are and why they matter, how and when to wash your hands properly, the rules for attire, jewelry, nails, and reporting illness, and how to move around the plant without carrying contamination from one area to another.

slides.title: What are GMPs?
slides.lead: The everyday rules that keep our food safe.

visual.caption: Basic conditions and habits an auditor expects to see on the floor, every single shift.

slides.notes: GMP stands for Good Manufacturing Practices. These are the basic rules and conditions that keep food safe while we make it — how we keep ourselves clean, how we dress, how we handle illness, and how we move through the plant. They're not extra paperwork; they're the foundation everything else is built on, and an auditor will look for them every shift.

slides.title: Why hygiene comes first
slides.lead: You are part of the process.
slides.notes: Here's the simple truth: people are the biggest source of contamination in a food plant. We naturally carry microbes on our hands, hair, skin, and clothing. Because we handle food directly, our personal hygiene is the first line of defense. Get this right and you stop most problems before they ever reach the product. That's why hygiene comes first.

slides.title: When to wash your hands
slides.lead: Wash at every one of these moments — no exceptions.

items: Before starting work and after every break
After using the restroom
After handling trash, cleaning, or touching your face or hair
Whenever you switch tasks or change zones

slides.notes: Knowing how to wash matters less if you skip the moments that count. Wash before you start and after every break. Always wash after using the restroom. Wash after handling trash, after cleaning, or after touching your face or hair. And wash whenever you switch tasks or move into a new zone. When in doubt, wash — it only takes a moment.

slides.title: How to wash — every time
slides.lead: The single most important habit in the plant.

steps: Soap & scrub 20 seconds — backs, between fingers, nails

slides.notes: Now the how. Wet your hands with warm water, then soap up and scrub for a full twenty seconds — that's about humming the happy birthday song twice. Don't forget the backs of your hands, between your fingers, and under your nails. Rinse clean, dry with a paper towel, and finish with sanitizer. Twenty seconds of scrubbing is what actually does the work.

slides.title: Dress for the job
slides.lead: Attire keeps you — and the product — protected.

items: Hair fully covered — hairnet, and a beard net if needed
Clean company uniform or smock; change it when soiled
Apron and sleeve covers where required
Street clothing covered; no loose threads or buttons

slides.notes: How we dress protects the food. Hair must be fully covered with a hairnet, and a beard net if you have facial hair, so nothing falls into the product. Wear a clean company uniform or smock and change it when it gets soiled. Put on an apron and sleeve covers where they're required. Keep street clothing covered, and watch for loose threads or buttons that could come off.

slides.title: Jewelry, nails & personal items
slides.lead: If it can fall off or hide dirt, it stays out.

items: Rings, watches, bracelets, earrings
False nails, nail polish
Phones and loose personal items
Cuts covered with a blue metal-detectable bandage
Wear a glove over a covered cut
Only items allowed by policy

visual.chip: When in doubt, leave it out

slides.notes: Anything that can fall off or trap dirt has to stay out of production. That means no rings, watches, bracelets, or earrings, no false nails or nail polish, and no phones or loose personal items on the line. If you have a cut, cover it with a blue, metal-detectable bandage and wear a glove over it. The only items you keep are the ones our policy specifically allows. When in doubt, leave it out.

slides.title: Report when you're sick

visual.label: REPORT
lines: Vomiting, diarrhea, fever, jaundice, or an infected cut or sore?
Tell your supervisor before you start — you may be reassigned or sent home.
You will never be in trouble for reporting — it keeps our food and your teammates safe.

slides.notes: This one protects everyone. If you have vomiting, diarrhea, a fever, jaundice, or an infected cut or sore, you must tell your supervisor before you start work. You might be reassigned away from open product or sent home — and that's okay. You will never be in trouble for reporting that you're sick. Coming in and staying quiet is what puts our food and your teammates at risk.

slides.title: Footwear & foot-bath sanitation
slides.lead: Keep what's on the floor from reaching the food.

items: Wear the dedicated plant footwear or boots provided
Step through the foot-bath or sanitizing station when entering
Keep boots clean; never wear outside shoes on the line
Report a foot-bath that looks empty or dirty

slides.notes: Floors collect everything we track in, so we keep that separate from the food. Wear the dedicated plant footwear or boots we provide — never your outside shoes on the line. When you enter, step through the foot-bath or sanitizing station so you're not carrying contamination in on your soles. Keep your boots clean, and if a foot-bath looks empty or dirty, report it so it gets refreshed.

slides.title: Move the right way between zones
slides.lead: Don't carry contamination from one area to another.

items: Dedicated tools and gowning
Enter clean — wash and sanitize first
Different stage, different tools
Don't backtrack into clean areas

visual.chip: Follow the flow — clean to dirty, never back

slides.notes: Our plant is laid out so product moves in one direction, from cleaner areas toward shipping. Higher-care zones have their own tools and gowning, and you enter them clean — washed and sanitized. General production and the warehouse are a different stage with different tools. The rule is simple: follow the flow from clean to dirty, and don't backtrack into clean areas carrying something from a dirtier one.

slides.title: Summary

items: GMPs are the everyday habits that keep our food safe
Wash hands the right way, at every key moment
Correct attire, no jewelry or polish, report illness
Move clean-to-dirty and never carry contamination between zones

slides.notes: Let's recap. Good manufacturing practices are the everyday habits that keep our food safe. Wash your hands the right way at every key moment. Wear the correct attire, leave jewelry and polish out, and always report illness. And move through the plant clean-to-dirty so you never carry contamination between zones. Up next we get to the heart of our program — allergens, and why they make our bakery different. Nice work finishing Module 2.

quiz.question: What does GMP stand for?
options: General Maintenance Protocol
Guided Material Processing
Grocery Market Permit
Good Manufacturing Practices
quiz.hint: It's about how we make the product, done well.
quiz.rationale: GMP = Good Manufacturing Practices — the basic habits and conditions that keep food safe.

quiz.question: How long should you scrub with soap when washing your hands?
options: At least 20 seconds
About 2 seconds
Until they look wet
Only after the restroom
quiz.hint: About two rounds of the happy birthday song.
quiz.rationale: A full 20 seconds of scrubbing — including backs, between fingers, and nails — is what actually removes microbes.

quiz.question: Which of these is a time you MUST wash your hands?
options: Only once at the very start of the working day
Only if your hands actually look visibly dirty to you
After the restroom, breaks, handling trash, and when changing zones
Only the burger team really needs to wash their hands
quiz.hint: It's more than one moment — think every transition.
quiz.rationale: Wash at every key moment: before starting, after breaks, after the restroom, after trash/cleaning/touching your face, and when switching tasks or zones.

quiz.question: You're about to start your shift. Which is acceptable in production?
options: A wedding ring together with a wristwatch on the line
A plain face with hair fully covered by a hairnet
Brightly painted fingernails or glued-on false nails on shift
A personal phone tucked into your apron pocket on the floor
quiz.hint: If it can fall off or hide dirt, it stays out.
quiz.rationale: Hair must be fully covered; jewelry, false/painted nails, and loose personal items like phones are not allowed on the line.

quiz.question: True or false: A little nail polish is fine as long as it isn't chipped.
options: True
False
quiz.hint: Polish can chip and flake into product without warning.
quiz.rationale: Nail polish and false nails are not permitted — they can flake or detach and become a foreign-material or contamination hazard.

quiz.question: You wake up with vomiting and diarrhea but you're scheduled to work the line. What should you do?
options: Come in to work anyway and just try to be careful
Take some medicine, say nothing, and work your shift
Wait to see if it gets worse during your shift
Tell your supervisor first; you may be reassigned or sent home
quiz.hint: Reporting protects the food and your teammates — and you won't be penalized.
quiz.rationale: Vomiting, diarrhea, fever, jaundice, or infected sores must be reported before starting. You may be reassigned or sent home, and you're never in trouble for reporting.

quiz.question: You get a small cut on your hand during the shift. What's required?
options: Cover it with a blue metal-detectable bandage, glove it, and report it
Keep working since the cut is really quite minor
Cover it with any clean tissue you can find and keep going
Just rinse it off under some water and carry on
quiz.hint: We use a specific bandage color for a reason.
quiz.rationale: Cuts must be covered with a blue, metal-detectable bandage and a glove, and reported — so a lost bandage can be detected and the wound can't contaminate product.

quiz.question: Why do we use dedicated footwear, foot-baths, and one-directional traffic between zones?
options: To keep the floors looking shiny and clean for visiting auditors
Because that whole approach is simply faster for the team
So we don't carry contamination or allergens into a cleaner area
Only to control who clocks in and out of each zone
quiz.hint: Think about what your boots and your path could carry with them.
quiz.rationale: Footwear control, foot-baths, and clean-to-dirty flow stop us from tracking contamination and allergens from general areas into higher-care zones.
